title: mfa — trait composition
description: One owned trait. `HasMfa` is a marker composed by services that participate in MFA policy resolution — actions that must be gated by a recent MFA challenge (sensitive-write actions, admin operations, platform-security surfaces) declare it so the boot-time discovery can enumerate them + wire the `mfa.require_recent_challenge` middleware automatically. NO BelongsToTenant on WebauthnCredential — WebAuthn credentials are GLOBAL (belong to the Identity), matching the substrate they extend.

owned.description: Marker trait for services + actions requiring MFA-gated access. Composition triggers a boot-time scan by the MfaServiceProvider that (a) enumerates every #[UsesTrait(HasMfa::class)] target, (b) verifies the class has a corresponding route mounted with `mfa.require_recent_challenge` middleware, and (c) fails the boot when a marker exists on a route missing the middleware. The trait itself contributes no methods — it is a pure discovery marker so the wiring stays declarative.
usedBy: identity/auth: EnrollWebauthnAction (registering a new credential requires a recent challenge on any existing MFA method)
identity/auth: DisableMfaAction (self-service disable requires a recent challenge on the very MFA method being disabled)

framework.reason: WebauthnCredential composes SoftDeletes so a `disabled_at` disable + eventual hard-delete after 90 days (CleanupDisabledWebauthnCredentialsJob) leaves an audit trail during the retention window. Consumers checking `is_active` read `disabled_at IS NULL`, not the SoftDeletes deleted_at.

notes: NO BelongsToTenant — WebAuthn credentials attach to the Identity, which is a global aggregate. A single physical hardware key is usable across every Application the Identity is provisioned into.
NO BelongsToApplication — same reason. The credential's public-key handle is Application-agnostic; the `X-Application-Id` header only affects which User row is resolved after MFA succeeds.
The trait order matters: `HasUlids` must precede `HasUserstamps` so the ULID is populated before the creator/updater columns fire on the observer chain.
`HasActivityLog` writes credential registration / rename / disable / delete to the shared `activity_log` table so the compliance timeline captures every hardware-key mutation with full attribution.
